title: Per-element reactive effects never stopped in long-lived indices
description: The entries Map stores ReactiveEffect objects for each element with reactive mapper dependencies. These effects are only stopped when: (1) the element is explicitly deleted via delete(), or (2) onCleanup runs at context disposal. For long-lived indices where not all elements are explicitly deleted, or where deletion is delayed, these effects accumulate and remain active, continuously tracking reactive dependencies and responding to mutations. This creates unbounded per-element effects for large sets.
evidence: Lines 249-254: entries Map stores effect references; onCleanup only runs at context disposal
Lines 280-287: effects stored in entry.eff only for reactive mappers (hasDeps=true)
Line 292: entry.eff?.stop() only called when delete() is explicitly called
No mechanism to garbage-collect entries for elements that remain in the set but are no longer accessed
impact: For symbol tables with hundreds of symbols where the mapper has reactive dependencies (e.g., OutputSymbol.refkeys, .name), the index maintains an effect per symbol that re-runs on any tracked state change. If a symbol is added but never deleted (common for global symbols), its effect persists for the lifetime of the application.
recommendations: Verify that all callers of createIndex guarantee delete() is called for all added elements
Consider adding weak references or an LRU eviction strategy for effect cleanup
Document the lifetime guarantee required from callers

title: Clever hasDeps detection lacks documentation
description: The static vs reactive mapper detection uses onTrack hook to set a boolean flag. This is an undocumented, non-obvious pattern that future maintainers may not understand.
evidence: Line 276: eff.onTrack = () => { hasDeps = true; }; — sets hook BEFORE first run
Line 277: eff.run(); — runs effect, hook fires if any deps tracked
Line 278: eff.onTrack = undefined; — clears hook after detection
impact: Medium - maintainers may incorrectly modify this pattern or misunderstand the optimization strategy
recommendations: Add a comment explaining: 'Detect if mapper has reactive dependencies by checking if onTrack was called during initial run. Static mappers (no deps) are stopped; reactive mappers keep the effect active.'

title: Unnecessary eff.onTrack cleanup
description: After running the effect and checking hasDeps, the code sets eff.onTrack = undefined. This is unnecessary since the hook is not used afterward.
evidence: Line 278: eff.onTrack = undefined; — only to zero it out
impact: Low - minor code smell, no functional issue
recommendations: Remove line 278; let the hook go out of scope naturally
Alternative: leave as-is for explicit cleanup semantics if that matches project conventions
